data.title: Accenture Q3: DOGE Problem Gets Worse
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Global consulting heavyweight Accenture (ACN) reported the third-quarter earnings report of their FY25 fiscal year, giving investors another opportunity to observe the company’s pivot towards GenAI.

Accenture’s management started reporting on its GenAI business over the past 15 months, and the Q3 earnings report showed GenAI bookings now totaled $5.1B, now accounting for 6.4% of the company’s consolidated bookings volume of ~$80B on a TTM basis. As noted in my previous coverage on Accenture, management was heavily leaning on an acquisition-led strategy to bolster its AI business and insulate itself from the global slowdown seen in consulting and managed services.

But the Q3 report also showed the contraction in quarterly bookings volume accelerated on a y/y basis. The slowdown in federal discretionary IT spending may have picked up pace, which hit its overall bookings volume, complicating Accenture’s pivot to GenAI in a tough macro environment for the company.

In my view, Accenture is still a high-quality company with a management that is resilient enough to navigate the company away from these headwinds. Unfortunately for the mid-term, I expect Accenture’s headwinds to persist.

This leads to reiterating my Neutral rating on the company.

Balancing The AI Pivot Vs. D.O.G.E Headwinds

I have been mostly bullish on Accenture’s pivot to GenAI over the past year. But in my last report I noted how the company’s debt-fueled strategy to acquire a significant cohort of startups in the GenAI space and get quick access to the AI market would weigh the company’s margins down.

Accenture’s Q3 report revealed GenAI bookings were up 67% to $1.5B in the third quarter versus Accenture’s consolidated bookings of $19.7B, decreasing 6% y/y. I will address the company’s acceleration in consolidated bookings later, but for now, On the Q3 earnings call, management revealed:

We are a leader in Gen AI with another milestone quarter of $1.5 billion in bookings and over $700 million in revenues, bringing our Q3 year-to-date GenAI bookings to a total of $4.1 billion and revenue to $1.8 billion.

If I factor in Accenture’s Q3 revenues of $17.7B and GenAI revenues of ~$700M, this implies GenAI accounted for 4% of the company’s consolidated revenues. In my view, this is not particularly impressive, but it is an encouraging sign that Accenture’s acquisition-led GenAI pivot is beginning to yield results. Note that on a TTM basis, Accenture’s GenAI bookings volume totaled $5.1B.

Circling back to Accenture’s Q3 revenues, the company recorded revenue growth of 7.5% y/y, beating consensus estimates by $380M, or ~2.2%. The sustained revenue growth pace in Financial Services and Health Services helped in pushing revenue growth for the quarter higher and recording one of the biggest revenue beats versus consensus estimates in over 12 quarters.

However, the accelerated contraction in Accenture’s bookings indicates the company’s inflection mode is going to take longer.

Q3 consolidated bookings of $19.7B contracted by 6%, faster than the 3% bookings contraction reported in the Q2 FY25 quarter. In the previous quarter, management explained on the Q2 FY25 call that Accenture’s Federal Services unit accounted for 8% of the company’s FY24 revenues of $64.9B, so the impact to revenues would be moderate. But on the Q3 call, management explained that they would be seeing a 2% headwind to Q4 revenues, which they expect to fall in the range of $17-17.6B.

Management noted that their book-to-bill ratio slumped to 1x, signaling a rapid slowdown in the volume of new business they receive versus the revenue they are able to record. The last time Accenture noted an at-par book-to-bill ratio was in September 2023 (Q4 FY23 call), when GenAI had firmly dug its heels into upending the macro environment for consulting firms like Accenture.

Management attributed the contraction in Accenture’s business to a slowdown in new procurement as well as cancellations of projects, which is alarming and may have led to a quicker contraction of Accenture’s future business volume. The government spending efficiency goals that D.O.G.E. had set through the first few months of Trump 2.0's term have resulted in many of Accenture’s federal services projects being cancelled. On top of that, I believe that the rapid back-and-forth in geopolitical tensions and the tariffs narrative may have also kept businesses on the sidelines as they tighten up their discretionary IT budgets and wait till the air clears. This will continue to add pressure to Accenture’s business outlook, in my opinion.

Gross margins improved sequentially by 304 bp to 32.9% but were still 49 bp below gross margins of 33.4% in Q3 of last year, implying business economics were slightly lower versus the previous year. GAAP operating margins rose 40 bp to 16.8% over the same quarter last year after accounting for the business optimization costs being incurred by Accenture. But the pressures in running its business operations may have pushed management to revise their outlook for operating margins to now land at the lower end of their previous target range of 15.6%-15.7%.

Still, the company reported GAAP EPS of $3.49, beating estimates by 17 cents a share.

Switching to Accenture’s balance sheet, I see that the company continues to maintain an iron-clad capital structure with $9.6B in cash+equivalents+ST investments versus $8.2B in debt and obligations.

Due to the tough macro, the company has decided to allocate a smaller share of their cash towards acquisitions, now expected to be $1-1.5B. Instead, what I believe management is now prioritizing is buying back its own shares at a faster pace.

In the Q3 earnings report, management repurchased “6.0 million shares for a total of $1.8 billion,“ the largest stock buyback executed by Accenture over the past three FYs, in dollar terms, per my analysis. Note that Accenture still has $3.3B in its share buyback program. If the company is accelerating its pace of share buybacks, this could indicate that management is seeing more value in buying back its own shares versus acquiring startups or allocating cash towards dividends.

In my view, this should eventually result in being bullish for Accenture, so an inflection should be around the corner.

Re-assessing My PT For Accenture, After Q3 Results

At the moment, management is looking towards single-digit growth in FY26, with the macro environment continuing to put pressure on the company as it keeps attempting to navigate away from its headwinds.

Markets are pricing in 7-8% of EPS growth in FY26 on the back of revenue expectations of 6-7% in the same year, while Accenture’s stock trades at ~21x FY26 earnings.

Observing the trend of Accenture’s forward PE, I see that the stock trades at a discount to its historical average of forward PE. I believe the discount is fair. Why?

Because, historically, Accenture has been able to demonstrate 8.5-9% growth in EPS over the past five years on a CAGR basis. Given this history, Accenture has usually traded around 26x forward earnings. But if the company is unable to match up to its historical trend of EPS growth and expects earnings to grow in the 7-8% range, I believe the company should actually trade at 22-23x forward earnings.

This implies ~7% scope for expansion in forward PE, pointing to a PT of ~$305 per share.

Given the range of headwinds that I expect to persist after reviewing Accenture’s Q3 earnings report, along with marginal scope for PE expansion, I believe a Neutral rating is warranted.

Takeaway

Accenture’s Q3 report showed strong growth in earnings and revenue as GenAI bookings continued to expand its presence in Accenture’s business.

But, with the US federal government downsizing its IT budget as well as IT departments worldwide reigning in their IT discretionary expenses, Accenture will continue to see some pressure before its path to AI inflection grows clearer.

For now, I recommend staying Neutral on Accenture.

data.title: 2 REITs To Avoid (The Raised Nail Gets Hammered)
data.description:
I once heard a friend of mine say, “The raised nail gets hammered”.

He was a wise friend who had made millions of dollars investing in boring businesses that were off the radar.

Most of his net worth was in real estate, cash, and collectibles.

He never chased shiny new toys, and he drove an old, worn-out Lincoln.

I could tell by his modest lifestyle that he never borrowed money, and he was one of the most frugal mentors that I know.

Needless to say, he was the opposite of flashy, and I knew that when he said, “the raised nail gets hammered,” he meant to avoid high-risk speculative investments.

These days, many high-yielding stocks offer tempting yields, ranging from 13% to 18%.

I typically avoid these gun-slinging stocks because their yields almost always mean that investors perceive very low growth, and even worse, a possible dividend cut.

As long as I’ve been writing on Seeking Alpha, I’ve tried my best to help readers navigate away from these dangerous ideas because they ultimately blow up and cost investors dearly.

If you don’t believe me, just take a look at the chart I prepared a few weeks ago that compares high-yielding residential mortgage REITs with commercial mortgage REITs and equity REITs:

As you can see, there’s a lot of “red ink” in the residential mREIT category, and you’d have to be an extremely skilled market timer to break even owning these securities.

That’s not to say that all stocks that yield 10% or higher are unsafe; there are rare occasions in which shares may be beaten down due to an overreaction or an out-of-favor sector.

But these instances are rare.

Just take a look at the list of “sucker yield” stocks that I recommended avoiding in July 2019:

The mall REITs – Washington Prime and CBL – were yielding 28% and 30%, respectively. I was the voice of reason in August 2019 as I explained,

“Before you invest hard-earned capital into WPG, CBL, PEI, and MAC, make sure that you recognize that all four REITs (in yellow) are becoming increasingly dangerous.”

These days, I continue to police the REIT sector, looking for dangerous dividend stocks to avoid. Typically, these types of articles don’t get much fanfare; however, if I can convince one reader to avoid losing hard-earned capital, I’m happy.

So, without further ado, let’s take a look at 2 REITs worth avoiding…

Sachem Capital Corp. (SACH)

Sachem Capital is a commercial mortgage REIT that originates, services, and manages a portfolio of short-term loans secured by first mortgage liens on commercial real estate property.

The $556mm of total assets includes 226 loans, totaling $457mm net of reserves in 15 states (although the bulk of the loans – around 78% - are located in CT, NY, and FL). The average loan size is $2.1 mm, and the company has completed over 2,000 loans (worth over $1B since inception. The average yield of the portfolio is 13.1%, and the distribution by property type is as follows:

  • Residential: 59.3%
  • Commercial: 28.0%
  • Land: 6.1%
  • Mixed Use: 6.6%.

Over the last few years, the residential lending industry has faced a host of headwinds, such as elevated interest rates, a slowdown in home sales, and tariff uncertainty, and this has made Sachem’s income less predictable.

In addition, real estate construction has impacted construction costs, which has disrupted the supply chain.

Furthermore, many borrowers are unable to refinance projects with conventional bank financing, which has led to kicking the can down the road for many borrowers.

As of Q1 '25, Sachem had $124 million of non-performing loans (NPLs) net, compared to $103 million of non-performing loans net as of December 31st, 2024. And as shown below, earnings per share have continued to decline, which is the primary reason for underperformance.

In the latest quarter, Sachem generated revenue of $11.4 million, a 31% decrease from Q1 '24. This is primarily due to the cumulative effect of fewer loan originations over the past 15 months, as well as elevated levels of non-performing loans and conversion of loans through foreclosure to real estate owned.

As of Q1 '25, Sachem has $81.8 million on unpaid foreclosures and $4.3 million in real estate owned.

Sachem has attempted to mitigate risk by diversifying into other property categories as well as partnering with Shem Creek, a CRE Finance platform that provides debt capital solutions to multifamily, workforce housing.

Also, through its Urbane New Haven partnership, Sachem hopes to build a pipeline of development projects where it can better control risk and returns.

Back in November 2024, Sachem whacked its dividend by 37.5%, and even after this large cut, shares are now yielding by 18.2%.

Clearly, Mr. Market remains alarmed that Sachem is not out of the woods and another dividend cut could be in store. And based upon consensus estimates, it appears that there’s a high probability that a cut is coming.

In short, Sachem has been a train wreck, and I would avoid this one at all costs. Perhaps Ladder Capital (LADR) – yielding 8.7% - or Starwood Property Trust (STWD) – yielding 9.6% - will quench your thirst for yield.

Gladstone Land Corporation (LAND)

Gladstone Land is an externally managed farming REIT that owns 150 farms totaling 103,000 acres in 15 states. States with the highest concentration include California (63 farms), Colorado (12 farms), and Florida (20 farms).

Total U.S. farmland value is approximately $3.4 trillion, which makes the value proposition for farm consolidation extremely attractive. The other pure-play farm REIT is Farmland Partners (FPI), which is internally managed.

Since its IPO in 2013, Gladstone Land has grown its portfolio from 1,631 acres to over 103,00 acres (including 55,000 acre-feet of water assets).

In general, I don’t favor externally managed REITs, but with Gladstone Land, I understand that size matters, and currently, with a market cap of around $365 million, it doesn’t make economic sense for the company to hire its own management team. Importantly, Gladstone has an experienced management team that owns 9% of the shares in Gladstone Land.

I’ve owned Gladstone Land over the years, but currently I’m bearish given the deteriorating fundamentals.

As you can see, AFFO per share continues to sink, which has led to an elevated payout ratio, seemingly a “sucker yield.” As you can see below, using FAST Graphs, Gladstone Land's 2025 payout ratio (using analyst consensus data) is 136% and goes to 143% in 2027.

This suggests that the yield is not sustainable.

Currently, shares are trading at $10.09 (was $40.36 in April 2022) with a dividend yield of 5.6%...

I’ll put it bluntly, I would not bet the farm on Gladstone Land.

Farmland Partners (ticker FPI) looks much more attractive with a payout ratio of 80% and appetizing growth in 2026 (+13%) and 2027 (+9%) – based on analysts' consensus data.

In Conclusion

Don’t swing at every pitch…

Wait for the perfect pitch to come down the plate…

Long-term REIT investors should be looking for companies with dividends that are not just safe, but also have good growth prospects.

Always look for REITs based on the stability and predictability of their operating cash flows, rents, built-in increases, and real estate operating costs.

And you remember what my friend said, “the raised nail gets hammered.”

Happy SWAN Investing!

data.title: Six Flags: FUNdamentally Strong, Undervalued, And Thrilling Initiatives
data.description:
Executive Summary

I initiate coverage on Six Flags Entertainment Corporation (NYSE:FUN) with a BUY recommendation for the amusement park operator.

With the stock badly bruised from a string of earnings misses in 2024, I believe that FUN is ripe for a recovery story. The market has overreacted to temporary attendance shortfalls while underappreciating FUN's revenue scalability, cost optimisation initiatives, and competitive edge.

Company Overview

Following its merger with fellow amusement park operator Cedar Fair, the new entity FUN generates revenue from admissions to amusement parks and water parks, food, merchandise, and games (both inside and outside the parks), as well as accommodations and extra-charge products.

FUN operates in North America with 42 parks continent-wide — 38 in the USA, 2 in Canada, and 2 in Mexico.

Industry Overview

The amusement parks industry is a moderately consolidated market, with major players including Walt Disney Co. (NYSE:DIS), Universal Destinations & Experiences (NYSE:UVV), FUN, United Parks & Resorts (NYSE:PRKS), and Merlin Entertainments.

Current tailwinds for the industry include adopting augmented reality and virtual reality in attractions to create more immersive experiences for visitors. Another important trend is the growing focus on themed attractions, particularly those based on popular media franchises, which are driving higher guest engagement and longer visits.

Trading Comparables Analysis

Choosing a peer group of other amusement park operators and adjacently related companies like resorts and cruise operators, FUN is arguably undervalued on an NTM EV/Revenue and EV/EBITDA basis, ranking slightly above the 25th percentile in both metrics despite measuring above the mean (and slightly below the median) on a LTM EBITDA margin basis, at 25.1%, and significantly above the highest (13.66%) when it comes to revenue growth, 54.72%.

However, FUN's NTM P/E multiple of 22.98x ranks above the median of 18.38x, indicating that the Street is cautious about FUN's NTM net income. While this pessimism might be attributed to FUN's LTM net income margin of -11.30%, I believe that the market has overcorrected FUN's share price and underestimated its scalability, competitive edge, and turnaround potential.

Investment Thesis

Catalyst 1 - The Street Underestimates FUN’s Scalability due to Weak Attendance

FUN missed certain Q1'25 estimates, with adjusted EBITDA of -$171M dismally below consensus estimates of -$125M, a reasonable cause for concern. To get more into the weeds, revenue fell 14% YoY, catalysed by a 7.1% decline in consolidated attendance within the same period while operating expenses came out 12% above analyst expectations.

Q1 consolidated attendance was slightly subpar compared to street estimates of 3.0M, with 1.6M and 1.2M legacy Six Flags guests and Cedar Fair guests respectively summing up to 2.8M consolidated visitors. Management attributed the modest decline to 1) a net decline of 14 operating days Y/Y due to “managing our park operating calendars tightly in response to inclement weather & other cost savings objectives” and 2) the Boysenberry Festival at Knott’s Berry Farm being delayed this year towards Q2 compared to last year to maximise attendance at Cedar Fair during the Easter holiday.

Hence, revenue is likely to recover in the near term due to increased visits during the Boysenberry/Easter Festive season as well as the lack of operational disruptions.

Management declared at the Q1'25 Investor Presentation: “Let me tell you, by 2028, we're targeting 58 million in attendance, almost 60 million, 58 million attendance, and $3.8 billion in revenue.”

While the 58 million attendance target might seem ambitious compared to 2024’s 50.2 million figure, I think it is achievable given that the current portfolio (excluding legacy Six Flags parks that have been phased out) attracted a little under 58 million visits in 2019. The visitor bleed due to COVID-19 has stemmed significantly (just shy of 8 million visitors), but FUN’s stock price remains moribund, down 38.02% YTD as of 14 June 2025.

The catalyst to reverse the market’s overcorrection is clear: increasing pass sales, fully recapturing lost visitors due to COVID, and encouraging revisits.

Having a larger seasonal pass base would ensure a steady stream of revenue while incentivising revisits from pass holders, which also encourages in-park spending. To realise this ambition, FUN aims to target parks with lower penetration, which they believe could attract 10 million visitors over the next few years. FUN’s business quality and ability to scale its customer base are key underappreciated elements. For reference, 15 parks account for 80% of their current consolidated attendance, with only 4 surpassing pre-COVID attendance levels thus far. Fully catching up to pre-COVID attendance figures coupled with investments in the 27 other parks which account for 10 million visitors would be significant in boosting admissions revenue.

FUN plans to invest approximately $1 billion in capital expenditures over the 2025 and 2026 seasons. The spending will cover ride upgrades, new attractions, system-wide restaurant renovations, new bundled products and collaborating with affiliated partners to provide park-exclusive merchandise. This two-pronged approach could 1) present a more convincing case to convert daily pass users to seasonal passes and 2) increase in-park per-capita spending, which took a 1.4% hit YoY (mainly due to the planned reduction in average season pass pricing).

Catalyst 2 - The Street is too Bearish on EBITDA Margin Recovery despite Cost Synergies and Optimisation

FUN’s Q1'25 adjusted EBITDA of -$171M tanked due to a $72.8M increase in COGS and a whopping 98% spike in SG&A expense from 2024 to 2023, inclusive of $107.9 million of additional expenses from Six Flags operations pre-merger and $61.5 million in transaction and integration costs from the merger.

FUN aims for an additional $60 million in cost savings for 2026, in addition to the $120 million in merger-related savings expected to be finalised by the end of 2025. Management describes this as a "total reset" of its cost structure, primarily affecting back-end and corporate operations. This initiative includes reducing the full-time workforce by roughly 10% and closing the legacy Six Flags corporate office in Texas.

Seasonal worker availability, which constitutes most of the operating expenses, is the highest in the last ten years. The company is also implementing strategies to reduce total work hours by repurposing staff into different roles as needed throughout the day.

Looking ahead, FUN anticipates further savings in 2026 as it renegotiates procurement and vendor agreements once legacy contracts expire. Moving forward, FUN wants increases in SG&A expenses to keep pace with inflation.

Furthermore, some recent expenses amounting to $10 million were due to timing differences that should correct themselves. This sum is mainly due to pushing forward pre-opening maintenance to prepare parks for opening day.

All in all, the non-recurring, temporal nature of the merger costs and cost-timing differences, together with FUN’s concrete cost-cutting strategies, will edge its EBITDA margin towards the 40% target by 2028.

Catalyst 3 - The Street Undervalues FUN’s Competitive Edge in Park Upgrades

FUN employs a "zone of impact" strategy, which ensures that major investments, like new thrill rides, are accompanied by upgrades to surrounding attractions, engendering higher Net Promoter Scores and repeat visitation. FUN also paces its growth CapEx, where large expenditures in one year are followed by smaller incremental investments the following year. Even during the 2008 financial crisis, FUN kept its investments up, leading to all-time high EBITDA at several parks.

Disney and Universal are more selective with their spending, emphasising themed attractions across their parks. Disney, for example, integrates stories into all its attractions, while Universal focuses on high-tech experiences. These approaches often prioritise specific areas of the park, while FUN aims for park-wide improvements.

FUN’s pacing strategy helps to sustain guest engagement over the long run, minimising cash burn as much as possible without hollowing out its business. Disney and Universal, by contrast, tend to make larger, more infrequent investments.

I believe that this is an overlooked, underlying strength of FUN's business model, as it is indicative of resilience during sector-wide downturns (especially in the case of a consumer discretionary industry). Should FUN and other amusement park operators experience weak attendance and hence low cash flows during such times of distress, FUN would have the least need for maintenance CapEx given that it regularly conducts area-wide upgrades. In contrast, its competitors who typically only conduct upgrades on an if-necessary basis might have to resort to raising external capital to conduct maintenance and necessary upgrades.

Conclusion

All in all, issues plaguing FUN in regard to visitor numbers and temporary higher operating costs are overblown. FUN can build consistent momentum following a (narrow) Q1 earnings beat, and follow through with its integration of Cedar Fair and realisation of the expected cost synergies to bolster its EBITDA margin. Its consistent, revenue-optimised investment strategy and its seasonal visitor pass promotion ensure a strong business model that makes revenue growth in the near future likely.

data.title: What Moved Markets This Week
data.description:
Listen on the go! A daily podcast of Wall Street Breakfast will be available by 8:00 a.m. on Seeking Alpha, iTunes, Spotify.

Wall Street on Friday ended largely flat for the holiday-shortened week, as market participants digested an escalating conflict between Israel and Iran and the Federal Reserve's fourth monetary policy decision of the year.

The fighting between Israel and Iran intensified this week, with both countries targeting each other with waves of missile strikes. Meanwhile, U.S. President Donald Trump said he will decide within two weeks whether the U.S. should launch an attack on Iran.

Interest rates took the spotlight on Wednesday. The Fed's monetary policy committee decided to keep the key policy rate on hold in a widely anticipated outcome. The committee's updated economic projections for 2025 still showed two rate cuts, but growth expectations were revised, and unemployment and inflation estimates were bumped up.

Fed Chair Jerome Powell underscored that the uncertainty stemming from tariffs was still extremely high. He said the central bank was well positioned to stick to a wait-and-see approach while keeping an eye out for the full effect of tariffs to show up in economic data.

For the week, the S&P (SP500) slipped -0.2%. The tech-heavy Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) added +0.2%, while the blue-chip Dow (DJI) ended flat. Read a preview of next week's major events in Seeking Alpha's Catalyst Watch.

data.title: RDVI: A Dividend ETF That Uses Options To Get Ahead
data.description:
Overview

FT Vest Rising Dividend Achievers Target Income ETF (RDVI) is an option ETF that provides exposure to high-quality dividend companies that have healthy financial metrics and have the ability to grow over time. Unlike many ETFs that utilize an option strategy, RDVI has demonstrated that it's capable of preserving investor capital. When I previously covered RDVI, I issued a buy rating because of this unique ability to provide stability. I wanted to revisit this ETF to discuss the utility around its dividend and provide a comparison of its performance against peers.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that RDVI's price has increased by about 2.6%. RDVI has been able to effectively recover from the initial market drop in reaction to President Trump's tariff proposals. When including all distributions paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up to 11.91% over the same period. The main appeal of RDVI is the generous starting dividend yield of 8.8%, which enables a larger stream of dividend income that can be collected from the underlying holdings.

RDVI's performance stands out because the fund has been able to outperform more traditional dividend ETFs. Option ETFs get a bad rep for their underperformance compared to these more traditional ETFs, so the fact that RDVI can significantly outperform peers like SCHD, makes it a strong contender in the world of high-yield funds. RDVI is still much newer than a lot of funds, with an inception dating back to October of 2022. However, the performance demonstrated so far has instilled confidence that the ETF will provide efficient returns with a buy and hold strategy. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying strategy that RDVI implements to generate its earnings for shareholders.

Fund Strategy

According to the latest fund breakdown, RDVI has total net assets of $1.9B that is spread across 77 different holdings. RDVI selects its holdings from the Nasdaq US Rising Dividend Achievers Index and filters through to search for companies that have favorable metrics. For instance, RDVI will only consider holdings that have favorable earnings growth, a healthy cash to debt ratio, and favorable dividend payout ratios. By only selecting companies with these favorable metrics, it limits exposure to companies that may underperform.

Another part of the strategy is to maintain an efficient level of diversity across sectors. We can see that Financials make up the largest bulk of assets at 31.26%. This is followed by exposure to Technology and Industrial investments, accounting for 20.13% and 16.88% of assets respectively. The combination of these top three assets makes up over 68% of RDVI's total assets, so we can assume that these sectors will have the greatest impact on RDVI's daily price movement.

I like the fact that RDVI still maintains a sizeable exposure to technology companies because it may allow the fund to participate in more capital appreciation over time. Option funds are known to deteriorate investor capital over time since the upside movement is capped. RDVI implements an ATM (at-the-money) option strategy against the S&P 500 Index as a way to generate additional income. ATM option strategies select a strike price relatively close to the current market price of the asset.

What allows RDVI to outperform is that it is currently writing options equivalent to 15.65% of its NAV. This means that it limits upside potential that can be capped and allows the fund to better participate in market appreciation. As a result, RDVI indicates that it captures approximately 84% of the upside movement of its underlying holdings.

So despite RDVI having exposure to many large-cap companies, I anticipate that it will underperform its own holdings over a longer time horizon. eBay (EBAY) is currently the largest holding with a weight of 2.53%. This is followed by exposure to other large-cap companies like Meta Platforms (META), The Bank of New York Mellon (BK), Unum Group (UNM), and Booking Holdings (BKNG) just to name a few.

Just to demonstrate the performance gap that an option strategy may cause, I pulled in a quick total return comparison between RDVI and some of its top holdings. As we can see below, RDVI's total return over the last twelve months is only 11.9%, which significantly underperforms all five of RDVI's top holdings over the same time frame. I anticipate this underperformance to continue over the long term due to the inclusion of an option strategy.

Comparison

There are a ton of different dividend focused ETFs for investors to choose from, each with a different strategy and selection process for its holdings. I believe that RDVI is highly competitive when thrown into the ring with these more traditional ETFs. This can be attributed to the fact that RDVI still implements a vigorous selection process for its holdings, even though it chooses to implement an option writing strategy anyways.

The flaw that some other dividend ETFs have is that they may put a lighter emphasis on choosing high-quality dividend stocks. Since most of the income that is being paid out to shareholders will come from option premiums, the thought process is that it becomes less important to focus on companies with the potential for dividend growth. RDVI separates itself because of its focus on quality and income generation. Some notable peers in the dividend ETF space would be the following:

  • iShares Core High Dividend ETF (HDV) - starting dividend yield of 3.5%.
  • Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD) - starting dividend yield of 3.9%.
  • Amplify CWP Enhanced Dividend Income ETF (DIVO) - starting dividend yield of 4.7%.

Each fund has a different underlying strategy and a range of starting dividend yields. RDVI has the highest dividend yield of the bunch and this helps lead to outperformance against the peer group. As we can see below, RDVI's total return of 53.8% since inception significantly outperforms the total return of the peer group.

While RDVI has outperformed so far, the downside is that the fund isn't likely to offer any sort of formal dividend growth over time. There just isn't a need to focus on dividend growth since the distributions are reliant on how successful the underlying option strategy is.

Rather than all of the distribution being funded by dividends and interest collected from the underlying holdings, the distributions are heavily reliant on the option premiums received. So if you are an investor that prefers to take the dividend growth route, you are better off with a more traditional ETF like SCHD.

Dividend Utility

As of the most recently declared monthly distribution of $0.1764 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 8.8%. Looking back on the dividend history, we can see that each of the monthly payouts are slightly different. Despite the slight variation in payouts, RDVI has been able to keep distributions in a pretty tight close range since its inception. This stability combined with the higher dividend yield, makes RDVI an attractive choice for income investors that prefer consistency and reliability. The first type of investor that comes to mind is the retired investors that depend on the income generated from their portfolio.

Although there isn't any type of formal dividend growth for RDVI, I believe that the consistent payout range can be a powerful tool for long-term holders. If RDVI is able to maintain these consistent payout amounts over time, investors will be able to essentially create their own dividend growth. This could be achieved by consistently investing new capital into your position or by reinvesting all dividends back into accumulating more shares of RDVI. By implementing either one of these strategies, investors will see their annual dividend income compound over time!

One of the main benefits of the income received from RDVI would be the strategic use of return of capital distributions. The latest section19(a) notice indicates that nearly 78% of the distributions that have been paid YTD, were done so using return of capital distributions. Return of capital gets a bad rep sometimes because they are typically used by closed end funds that are underperforming and aren't generating enough income to actually support the distributions they pay. However, the use of return of capital here is not harmful since RDVI's NAV has increased since inception.

Return of capital distributions are tax-efficient since the dividends aren't technically considered to be income. Rather than paying taxes on the distributions, return of capital reduces an investor's cost basis and this allows taxes to be deferred until the time of sale.

For example, if you buy a stock at $10 per share, and it pays out a $1 dividend using return of capital, your cost basis will be reduced down to $9 per share. So it's possible to maintain a long-term position until your cost basis is reduced to $0 per share. At this point, all distributions received will be classified as long term capital gains.

Therefore, RDVI can be a tax-efficient choice for investors that want to limit their tax liability at the end of every calendar year. This structure also allows RDVI to be efficiently used within a regular taxable brokerage account. However, it's important to note that approximately 22% of the distribution YTD has still been funded by net investment income and short-term capital gains. So this smaller portion of the distributions paid will still have tax consequences that investors should consider. Albeit, the tax consequences here are still more favorable compared to a scenario where a fund is paying distributions using 100% net investment income.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I maintain my buy rating on RDVI due to its strategic use of options that still allow for price appreciation to take place. RDVI combines a specific selection process with its option strategy, which results in the fund providing exposure to some of the highest quality dividend paying companies in the world. The underlying strategy also maintains a sizable exposure to technology companies, which helps the price grow over time. RDVI's strategy is proven when we compare the total return performance against some notable dividend ETF peers. The payouts have remained fairly consistent since inception and the stable payouts can enable investors to create their own dividend growth over time. The fund puts an emphasis on return of capital distributions, which are tax-efficient and makes this a great choice for investors that want to limit tax liabilities.

data.title: Palantir: Another Valuation Attempt
data.description:
In my last Palantir (NASDAQ:PLTR) coverage in February, I argued that mathematically, it is of course possible to create a Discounted Cashflow model that could justify a market cap in the hundreds of billions of dollars with only single-digit billions of revenues. However, the necessary assumptions used were highly optimistic. The optimistic model’s spot estimate for Enterprise Value was $171 billion, translating to an Equity Value of $176 billion and indicating 28% downside from its market capitalization of $244 billion at that time. Comically, Palantir dropped almost exactly that, by minus 27% before surging to all-time highs once again.

As the company’s fundamentally impressive performance is undisputed, it is all about valuation. This time, moving away from the DCF model, I try a different approach to make sense of Palantir’s valuation, if that is even possible.

Scenario 1: Treating Palantir As A Mag-7 Company

Let us assume that Palantir is a company that fundamentally elevates to a state equaling those of today’s group of the Magnificent 7 stocks in the future. This approach assumes that as soon as Palantir reaches the stage of today’s Mag 7 in the future, it will be valued at similar multiples at that point in time. Comparing this with Palantir’s today’s multiples then allows us to project what fundamental metrics the company must eventually reach. Note that this approach is almost ridiculously optimistic, as it completely ignores the time value of money, ignoring the risk that future actuals will divert from these target metrics.

Read the table below as follows: The median of Mag 7 adjusted P/S currently stands at 10.6, while Palantir’s is 114.7 given a market capitalization of $357 billion and TTM revenues of $3.12 billion. If Palantir were to eventually also be valued at 10.6x sales, as is the median of today’s Mag 7, Palantir’s sales must at least increase 11x to $33.71 billion.

Applying the same approach to earnings, for example, reported earnings must at least increase 18x and adjusted earnings must at least increase 12x. These metrics must be hit in the future under these assumptions, as, again, time value of money is disregarded here.

Also note that Palantir’s current P/E, adjusted P/E and P/S of 611, 304 and 115 are well above its own ten-year-average of 311, 147 and 38.

Scenario 2: Fundamentals Not So Different From Nvidia’s

Next, note that among all Mag 7, there is one whose growth rates resemble those of Palantir. Nvidia’s historical 5-year as well as 1-year estimated revenue and earnings growth rates match or even exceed those of Palantir. One might therefore even compare Nvidia’s multiples to Palantir’s based on today’s fundamentals, as opposed to future ones like in the first Scenario. Here is how to read this table: Nvidia currently trades at 24.1x sales, while Palantir’s P/S is 114.7, implying that Palantir’s target revenue should even be $14.83 billion already, while it is still 5x lower at $3.12 billion.

Applying the same approach to earnings, reported and adjusted earnings should theoretically already be 13 times or 7 times higher than they are to justify the stock’s valuation.

While these are two highly simplified scenarios, they depict the magnitude of expectations priced into Palantir by quantifying the factors the company’s fundamentals should multiply by in order to justify these prices.

Scenario 3: Compounding The DCF

Finally, let us return to last time’s optimistic DCF Equity Value of $176 billion, resulting from an Enterprise Value of $171 billion and Net Cash of $5 billion. My WACC calculation for Palantir was around 14-15% last time and 4 months have passed (1/3 of a year), so let us simplify and compound $176 billion by 5%. With that, the compounded DCF from February would now stand at $185 billion. Again, accounting for the positive momentum, I would like to call anything around or below the $200 billion threshold as interesting, implying 40% downside from today’s $330 billion, or a stock price below $90.

Impressive, Yet Not Flawless Fundamentals

After reporting Q1-2025, Palantir raised its 2025 full-year guidance to 36% revenue growth, now eying a mid-point of $3,896 million, up 3% from the base assumption of $3,790 million in my last article. The adjusted FCF margin is expected to be 44%. My DCF model has also already incorporated increased FCF margins of 45%, eventually reaching 50% at a later stage.

What appears sobering is that despite boasting an expected 68% U.S. commercial growth for 2025, revenue growth remains at 36%. This indicates relatively weaker international demand. The same applies to Q1. Despite 71% US commercial growth and 45% US government growth, the top line only grew 39%, indicating international restraint. An observation that would not even be overly surprising amid global efforts to partly reduce US dependence.

Adjusted FCF margin was 42% while, again, the DCF model assumes 45%. Furthermore, the margin in the model is an unadjusted FCF margin and is therefore in two ways more optimistic than the actual.

Cash and equivalents are 81% of the company’s balance sheet, adding a source of non-operating income, while on the other hand, share count is ever increasing, leading to dilution. However, at these valuation levels, both effects are almost negligible.

Programmed For Volatility

Amid these valuation levels, it is impossible to assign a Buy on Palantir, while at the same time its strong fundamentals and potential as well as momentum do not warrant a Sell rating either. However, highlighting the US growth in its earnings report indicates weakening international performance. Palantir remains a stock where a fast 30-40% decline would not be statistically unusual at any given time to levels, where new investors might cautiously initiate a small high-risk position.

data.title: Lithium Miners News For The Month Of June 2025
data.description:
Welcome to the June 2025 edition of the lithium miner news.

The past month saw lithium prices lower, touching a 4-year low. At these low prices, almost half the producers are unprofitable, suggesting we should see something given sooner rather than later.

Lithium price news

Metal.com reported a lithium spodumene concentrate Index (Li2O 5.5%-6.2%, excluding tax/insurance/freight) spot price of USD 620, as of June 20, 2025, lower again the past month.

The China lithium carbonate spot price was CNY 60,400 the past month. The CIF Asia lithium carbonate price was ~US$8/kg.

China lithium carbonate spot price 5-year chart - CNY 60,400 (~USD 8,402) (source)

Lithium demand versus supply outlook

BloombergNEF forecasts lithium deficits may begin in 2025 (as of Oct. 2024) (source)

RMIS forecasts lithium deficits to start after 2028, and may grow very large without major new investments (as of May 2025) (source)

Trend Investing vs. IEA demand forecast for EV metals (IEA)

Lithium market and battery news

In May, Katusa Research wrote:

80% Crash, 700% demand growth: This sector’s math doesn’t add up...The math is brutal: lithium demand needs to triple again by 2035, but new mine approvals have stalled. Something has to give. Supply, meanwhile, must add 55 average-sized new mines just to stay even in the IEA’s conservative scenario. Track offtake deals and construction slowdowns. When you see both in the same week, the breakout clock starts—and the first 100-300% gains will come lightning fast.

On May 18, China Daily reported: "China's Gotion launches first road tests for all-solid-state batteries."

On May 27, the Market Index reported:

Rise of the machines: How 1 billion humanoid robots by 2050 will drive a surge in critical minerals demand. Major broking and research firm Morgan Stanley has just released new modeling suggesting a massive increase in “humanoid” robots by 2050. This increase will inevitably require huge amounts of critical minerals, with rare earth minerals, lithium, copper, nickel, cobalt, and graphite at the forefront of booming demand...the potential big winners among these critical minerals become most apparent.

  • NdPr: Supply deficit of 13% by 2040; 26% by 2050

  • Lithium: Supply deficit of 75% by 2040; 78% by 2050

  • Copper: Supply deficit of 0.6% by 2040; 1.5% by 2050

  • Nickel: Supply deficit of 17% by 2040; 25% by 2050

  • Cobalt: Supply deficit of 16% by 2040; 34% by 2050

  • Graphite: not modeled...

On May 30, Cars Guide reported:

Forget solid-state batteries, another major electric car battery breakthrough. Chinese battery giant CATL has announced a major battery breakthrough in its lithium metal battery [LMB] technology that surpasses the theoretical energy efficiency of some solid-state batteries. It is still in the prototype phase, but CATL...claims to have developed an LMB battery with an optimised energy efficiency of 500kWh/kg maintained over 483 charging cycles...Unlike solid-state batteries, which use a solid electrolyte, CATL's LMB batteries use a liquid electrolyte...

In June, Fastmarkets announced:

Lithium: Market on edge, spodumene price declines accelerate. Lithium prices remain under extreme pressure as the downward trend dives deeper into the cost curve...Prime candidates for additional cuts are higher-cost Australian miners and African projects – who will blink first?

On June 6, CNEVPOST reported:

Global EV battery market share in Jan-Apr 2025: CATL 38.1%, BYD 17.3%... CATL's market share slightly decreased from January to April compared to January to March, while BYD's slightly increased...Global EV battery usage totaled 308.5 GWh in January-April, up 40.2 percent from 220.1 GWh in the same period last year.

CATL and BYD combined have 55.4% of the global EV battery market (source)

On June 10, Reuters reported:

Zimbabwe to ban export of lithium concentrates from 2027...as it extends its push for more local processing...Sinomine and Zhejiang Huayou Cobalt are part of a group of Chinese firms, including Chengxin Lithium Group, Yahua Group, and Canmax Technologies, which have spent more than $1 billion since 2021 to acquire and develop lithium projects in Zimbabwe.

On June 12, CSIS wrote:

Building a new market to counter Chinese mineral market manipulation... A major challenge to achieving mineral security is China’s manipulation of global markets, whereby Chinese companies flood the market with excess supply, driving prices down to levels that force mining operations in countries like the United States and Australia to shut down...In light of China’s aggressive use of export controls, building supply chains for critical minerals with allied nations is more crucial than ever. However, this cannot be done without market intervention, given China’s flooding of the market...the United States and its allies cannot afford to act in isolation. Unilateral efforts—whether through tariffs, subsidies, or investment restrictions—will remain insufficient given the relatively small market share of individual countries. Instead, building a unified anchor market that aligns with the policies of like-minded nations is the only realistic path to confronting China’s dominance. By harmonizing tariffs, establishing collective quotas, and coordinating investment protections, the anchor market can shift leverage away from Beijing and toward a more resilient, rules-based minerals ecosystem.

On June 13, Todd Clewett (External Affairs Executive) announced on LinkedIn:

There's $2,500,000,000 in the 2025 Budget Reconciliation Bill for the US production of critical minerals through the National Defense Stockpile. There's a further $500,000,000 for the Dept of Defense Credit Program for loans, loan guarantees, and technical assistance for the development of reliable sources of critical minerals, both domestically and internationally. Congress is likely to pass the Bill (in some form) by the end of July. This is going to happen fast.

Conclusion

China lithium carbonate spot prices and spodumene spot prices were lower in the past month.

data.title: Nike: Not Priced For Disappointment
data.description:
Nike, Inc. (NYSE:NKE) is a prime example of a company where the market overpaid so much for the stock that even after falling over $100 from the all-time highs a shareholder still doesn't have a bargain. The athletic footwear company still trades at a premium valuation with limited growth ahead due to a tough competitive environment, normal of a period of rich valuations. My investment thesis remains Bearish on the stock with the market still not fully appreciating the downside risk.

Huge Competition

The biggest issue facing Nike is the competition. On Holding (ONON) is likely the biggest threat, along with HOKA owned by Deckers Outdoor (DECK).

On Running is a hot running footwear company set to reach $3.6 billion in annual revenues this year at nearly 40% growth. Deckers doesn't have the same overall growth, but HOKA is a major threat in the footwear category.

Now, even Nobull is becoming a threat to Nike with the backing of Tom Brady. For now, it's just a random athletic footwear/apparel company, but it's a prime example of how Nike will continue struggling to grow with such intense competition.

According to Neustreet, just as big of an issue for Nike is the reduction in the secondary market for premium footwear. In fact, signs exist that Nike contributed to the problem by overselling these premium shoes causing prices to collapse after the big Covid boost in collectibles.

According to Statista, Nike saw footwear sales surges from $23 billion annually at the start of Covid to over $35 billion in 2023. The prime competitor in adidas (OTCQX:ADDYY) didn't see sales grow during this period, suggesting Nike probably saw some strong growth from secondary market demand that wasn't sustainable.

Falling Numbers

Nike plans to release FQ4 '25 results on June 26 with the following consensus estimates:

  • Revenue of $10.7 billion, down 15% YoY
  • EPS of $0.12, down 89% YoY

Investors constantly got into trouble over the last few years due to the company reporting big quarterly beats while the numbers were actually bad. Nike hit nearly $48 billion in TTM revenues back at the end of 2022 and revenues are targeted at staying at only $45 billion going forward through at least FY26.

The crazy part is that Nike reported FQ4'24 sales down nearly 2% last June, yet the stock ran to nearly $90. The company was only earning $3 per share and guided to very weak FQ1 '25 results with a mid-single digit dip forecast.

The key is that the market still doesn't appear to have come to grips with the new reality for Nike. The athletic apparel company faces macro weakness and tariff issues to end FY25, but the stock still trades at a more normalized 23x FY27 EPS targets of $2.64.

Nike is getting a huge benefit of doubt to assume the company comes back and boosts EPS by 40% in a few years. Analysts forecast an EPS of only $1.90 in FY26, though the forecast could include some tariff hits that likely aren't drastic outside of any reliance on China.

The company is forecast to mostly report FY26 revenues inline with last year. Either way, Nike isn't forecast to grow revenues, yet the stock trades at multiples above the market.

Investors shouldn't view the stock as down $100 from the Covid peak. Nike should be viewed based on the readily available data of a massive athletic footwear company that hasn't grown in years facing immense competition.

Nike has the potential to get EPS back to the $2 to $3 range, but the stock already trades at 20x the high-end targets. If the competition continues to grow and Nike bleeds market share, the stock might struggle to maintain a $2 EPS.

Either way, an investor has to make sure one doesn't latch onto another quarterly beat, instead focusing on the guidance provided in the earnings call. Nike is likely to provide weak guidance again and the market focus will ultimately lean towards guidance, not where the company reports another quarterly EPS beat topping $0.10.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that Nike is forecast to report a horrible quarter this upcoming week. The stock might be down substantially from the all-time highs a few years ago, but Nike isn't actually trading like the business is under pressure.

Investors should continue selling the stock while still trading at a premium valuation.

data.title: Dutch Bros Is Growing Fast, So Is The Market Cap
data.description:
Dutch Bros (NYSE:BROS) continues to impress with rapid expansion and strong revenue growth, but the stock looks like it’s already sprinted a bit ahead of the fundamentals. I’m encouraged by the company’s operational performance, particularly with the positive same-store sales and the fast-paced rollout of new locations. However, after nearly doubling over the past year, shares now trade near all-time highs and at valuation multiples far above even best-in-class peers like Starbucks (SBUX) and McDonald’s (MCD). If we add to that the continued reliance on share issuance to fund growth, the investment case starts to feel stretched. It’s a great business, no doubt, but at this price, it is only going to be worth it if you are a truly long-term investor.

Another Great Quarter

Let me start by highlighting the impressive top-line strength. Revenue in Q1 sat at $355.2 million, a 29.1% increase year-over-year. That growth was driven by continued shop openings and a bounce in same-store sales. In the last quarter, Dutch Bros opened 30 new shops, pushing the total to 1,012, after opening its 1000th shop on February 7 in Orlando, Florida.

Same shop sales showed a significant uplift, growing by 6.9%, up from the prior quarter, which tells me that demand is holding up despite some of the shakier macro backdrop we have seen recently. For me, this is quite some achievement given that a titan such as Starbucks reported U.S. comparable store sales declining 2%. According to management, this improvement was a result of better labor scheduling, stronger promotions, and with the help of a smoother digital ordering experience, which all boosted throughput and ticket size. The introduction of more streamlined beverage production practices and optimized menu displays also contributed to throughput gains, particularly during peak hours.

Profitability also showed signs of progress in my opinion. Adjusted EBITDA grew 19.7% to $62.9 million, and GAAP net income was $22.5 million. The company ended the quarter with adjusted EPS of $0.14, compared to $0.09 in Q1 of last year.

However, not all of it was roses in that 3 months. The input costs have remained elevated, and while management has made progress on inventory discipline and scheduling efficiency, the benefits have not yet translated into broader margin expansion.

Some of those cost headwinds are starting to bite into the operational efficiency gains. Shop-level margins for company-run stores held at 21.9%, about flat versus last year, largely as a result of the 100 basis points increase in labor cost for the quarter. Yet, I think the scale story largely remains intact, as we are about to see the best of the business.

Expansion Is on Track

So far, Dutch Bros is executing the playbook very well. Management reaffirmed the target of 160 new shops in 2025, and the pace suggests they’re on track. Unit economics also look solid, with AUVs above $2 million in the mature base and new shop paybacks around two years. In several western states, payback periods have even dipped below 20 months, signaling robust local demand and operational efficiency. That number could even drop further if the management succeeds in dropping the costs of opening stores from $1.7 million in 2024 to $1.25 million in the future.

They’re also clearly investing in the systems needed to support scale. There has been a lot of effort dedicated to better training, retention initiatives, and digital upgrades. The recently implemented workforce management software has started to yield better labor utilization metrics, and new broista onboarding programs are improving customer satisfaction scores. I still think the “broista” culture is a real asset, especially in their core Western markets, where the brand has deep roots and high recognition.

However, as Dutch Bros pushes eastward, I’m starting to question how well that culture can scale. Management downplayed the softer-than-expected ramp-up in some newer Southeastern markets, but I’m watching that closely. Not every region will mirror the early success of Arizona or Oregon, as you can see from the picture below.

I also wonder whether the brand's heavy reliance on the drive-thru format can achieve the same loyalty and throughput in colder or more urbanized climates where drive-thru usage may be lower.

Confident Guidance

Management’s guidance paints a picture of confidence, but I’m more cautious.

The long-term roadmap to 2,029 locations by 2029 looks well within reach, if the business increases the store openings each year by about 20%, which is not an easy task at all. Since about two-thirds of the shops are company-operated, Dutch Bros gets to control more of the customer experience, but that also means it bears more of the labor and operational burden, too.

If we look at the numbers provided in the last report, the guidance for the full year remains unchanged. Revenue is still expected between $1.15 billion and $1.17 billion, and adjusted EBITDA should land between $205 million and $215 million. Capex guidance remains elevated as well, with most of the spend tied to real estate and construction for new shops, commissaries, and regional distribution hubs. Free cash flow is still expected to be negative for 2025, with 2026 expected to be the first real turning point.

Overall, the CEO, Christine Barone, highlighted the company’s ability to adapt if inflation or regional softness emerges. The executive team believes that improvements in supply chain reliability and vendor consolidation efforts will help support margins even better in the second half of the year. But as the expansion continues at this pace, and profitability remains a few years out, the valuation starts to matter more. With shares already rallying sharply over the past year, the question becomes not whether Dutch Bros can grow into its goals, but whether that growth is already more than priced in.

A Premium That’s Hard to Justify

Shares of Dutch Bros have surged about 76% over the past 12 months and are up over 33% year-to-date, making it one of the top-performing names in the restaurant and beverage space. Outperforming titans such as Starbucks and McDonald's by as much as 60% in the last year.

The stock is currently leaning towards the high end of its historical range and hovers not far from the average analyst price target of $78, implying limited upside from here. Especially when we look at what multiples the business is trading at.

What makes this more difficult to reconcile is the sheer magnitude of the valuation premium. Dutch Bros trades at nearly 117x forward earnings, numbers that far exceed what even best-in-class consumer discretionary companies typically command. Starbucks, for example, trades at 37x forward earnings, McDonald’s at 23.6x, and even newer growth names like Luckin Coffee fetch far lower multiples, such as 21x net earnings, relative to their growth trajectories and margin profiles.

The same is true if we compare the business based on a forward EV/EBITDA basis, where Dutch Bros trades at over 35x. That’s way higher than Starbucks at 22x, and nearly double McDonald's 17.8x. But here we have to take into account how fast the business is growing, 6% same-store sales growth compared to negative 2% for Starbucks, as mentioned before.

It’s not that Dutch Bros doesn’t deserve a premium, I am pretty sure it does. After all, the business seems to be doing much better than its peers, despite not having their scale yet. The question is how much of a premium is too much.

That’s why I ran a projection based on Dutch Bros’ own growth targets. By 2029, the company plans to reach 2,029 stores across the United States. If we assume that the business manages to add another 15% increase in average unit sales over the next five years (factoring in inflation and operating improvements), revenue could potentially reach roughly $3.04 billion. That estimate is slightly below the analyst consensus range of $3.24 billion to $3.59 billion, with a midpoint of $3.41 billion.

Supporting that topline growth is a margin structure that continues to improve. According to the data, Dutch Bros has already surpassed Starbucks on gross margin, and its EBITDA margin currently stands at about 15.4%. By 2029, I believe Dutch Bros could reasonably achieve EBITDA margins north of 20%, especially with the planned introduction of food offerings beginning in fiscal 2026, however we would use the more conservative 20% EBITDA margins for our valuation. On $3.04 billion in revenue, that would translate to about $608 million in EBITDA.

Using Starbucks’ current EV/EBITDA multiple as a base and adding a bit of a premium, I modeled the potential market cap for Dutch Bros in 2029. Even after accounting for the recent run-up in the stock and assuming no further multiple expansion, the company still has room to potentially double its market capitalization over the next five years. That would represent a CAGR of 15%, which is a very decent return, but nothing extraordinary at this point, especially when viewed against the elevated expectations currently embedded in the stock.

That said, it's important to distinguish between company value and shareholder return. My estimates speak to where the business could be valued in five years, but that doesn’t guarantee similar upside for the stock. And that brings us to what really concerns me about the stock.

Risks: Scaling Costs, Share Dilution, Tariff Exposure, and Market Saturation

Dutch Bros faces a unique set of risks, which I will start pointing out one by one.

First is the cost structure. Scaling at this pace, 200 stores per year on average over the next five years, comes at a price. In the past three years, Dutch Bros has been issuing a significant number of shares. To be precise, in 36 months, shares in issue have grown about 2.5x. That kind of dilution is hard to ignore. Even if Dutch Bros were to slow the pace and double its share count “only” by 2029, that dilution alone could significantly diminish shareholder returns, even if operating results continue to improve.

Tariffs are the most pressing near-term risk. The company hasn’t raised prices yet, but if import costs increase, it may have to eat the hit or pass it on to customers, neither of which is ideal in a competitive market. Unlike Starbucks, which operates a far more global and diversified business with a more balanced revenue mix and lower capital intensity, Dutch Bros remains highly concentrated in the U.S. That exposes it more directly to U.S. wage inflation, food input volatility.

We also cannot ignore the question about long-term saturation. The company’s goal of 7,000 units isn’t implausible, considering Starbucks, which has already surpassed 16,000 stores in the U.S. Furthermore, while performance in the West has been strong, newer Southeastern markets are still ramping slowly. If the brand pushes into regions that lack the same drive-thru culture or customer familiarity, systemwide AUVs could decline over time. Those risks seem a bit more distant, but they will grow more relevant in the next decade.

Lastly, valuation itself is also a risk. There’s a clear path to underperformance if any piece of the story stumbles. With expectations so high, the market won’t be forgiving, and any miss, whether that would be on same shop sales, margins, or unit openings, it could result in a sharp re-rating and significant multiples contraction.

Great Company, At a High Price

Dutch Bros remains an impressive business with enormous potential. It’s one of the most capable operators in the U.S. beverage space, with strong brand equity and a compelling long-term growth strategy. But the continued pace of share dilution makes it difficult to forecast reliable per-share returns. I’d welcome more clarity from management, particularly a timeline for when equity issuance will slow or stop. That kind of signal would go a long way in restoring confidence around long-term value creation.

In my view, Dutch Bros is best suited for patient, long-term investors who are comfortable with short-term volatility and willing to wait for operating leverage.

data.title: Allete: Delay In Transaction Closing, 14% ROR Opportunity
data.description:
ALLETE: Investment Thesis

On Feb. 7, 2025, I published an article, "ALLETE, Inc.: 11% Return Beats A Bank For Short-Term Idle Cash". I included a copy of an Excel spreadsheet showing how a purchase of ALLETE stock could provide a fairly assured ~11% return through June 30, 2025, assuming receipt of all necessary approvals and close of the share sale transaction by that date.

The share sale transaction is now not expected to close until later in 2025 and due to the extended duration, any share purchases back in February 2025 will likely have a reduction in potential rate of return to slightly better than mid single digits. At the same time, additional share purchases around the current share price level can be expected to achieve double-digit rates of return, providing the transaction closes by the end of 2025.

Background

In my June 30, 2024, article, "ALLETE, Inc.: BlackRock's Backdoor Takeover", with the share price at $62.57, I discussed how shareholders in ALLETE could earn 12.3% per annum by holding through mid-2025. This was predicated on a successful outcome of the bid for all the shares of the company at $67 cash per share expected to settle in mid-2025, subject to shareholder and various regulatory approvals. The offer also indicated dividends would continue to be paid through to settlement.

Excerpted from the ALLETE, Inc. (NYSE:ALE) 2023 Annual Report,

On May 6, 2024, ALLETE and a partnership led by Canada Pension Plan Investment Board (“CPP Investments”) and Global Infrastructure Partners (“GIP”), (the “partnership”), jointly announced that they have entered into a definitive agreement under which the partnership will acquire ALLETE for $67.00 per share in cash, or $6.2 billion including the assumption of debt (the "Merger"). The transaction is expected to close in mid-2025 and is subject to the approval of ALLETE’s shareholders, the receipt of regulatory approvals including by the Minnesota Public Utilities Commission ("MPUC"), the Public Service Commission of Wisconsin ("PSCW") and the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission ("FERC"), and other customary closing conditions.

On Feb. 7, 2025, I published article, "ALLETE, Inc.: 11% Return Beats A Bank For Short-Term Idle Cash". I included a copy of an Excel spreadsheet showing how a purchase of ALLETE stock could provide a fairly assured ~11% return through June 30, 2025, assuming receipt of all necessary approvals and close of the share sale transaction by that date.

Status of Required Regulatory Approvals for Proposed Transaction

On December 19, 2024, the company filed an 8-K announcing,

ALLETE receives FERC approval for the proposed transaction with the Canada Pension Plan Investment Board and Global Infrastructure Partners. Expects transaction to close in mid-2025.

Since publication of above linked article, ALLETE announced on March 13, 2025, approval of the proposed transaction by the Public Service Commission of Wisconsin, and further advised, "... ALLETE continues to work on securing the last major regulatory approval for the transaction from the Minnesota Public Utilities Commission (MPUC)... ALLETE expects to complete the transaction in mid-2025, subject to remaining regulatory approvals and other customary closing conditions. "

First Indication of Delay to Close of Proposed Transaction

At the ALLETE 2024 Annual Meeting on 13 May 2025, ALLETE CEO, Bethany Owen, advised the share sale transaction remains on track for a 2025 closing, subject to all necessary approvals. The CEO further advised that the regulatory process with the Minnesota Public Utilities Commission ("MPUC") continues, and we expect a meeting before the Minnesota Public Utilities Commission later this fall.

Fall in the United States will begin on September 22nd, 2025, so the proposed transaction can not now close until sometime during Q4-2025.

Share Sale Transaction Termination Rights

Excerpted from 6 May 2024 8-K filing,

The Merger Agreement contains certain termination rights for both the Company and Parent, including if the Merger is not consummated by August 5, 2025 (subject to extension for an additional two successive three-month periods if all of the conditions to closing, other than the conditions related to obtaining regulatory approvals, have been satisfied). The Merger Agreement also provides for certain termination rights for each of the Company and Parent, and provides that, upon termination of the Merger Agreement under certain specified circumstances, Parent would be required to pay a termination fee of $212 million or $164 million (depending on the specific circumstances of termination) to the Company, and under other specified circumstances, the Company would be required to pay Parent a termination fee of $116 million.

The extension provisions allow for the closing to be deferred beyond August 5, 2025 out to February 5, 2026, so there does not appear to be any cause for concern at present the transaction might not proceed.

Termination by the Company (ALLETE)

As the shareholders have approved the sale, I expect this is no longer an option.

Termination by the Parent (buyer)

ALLETE has ~58 million shares on issue, so if the Parent (buyer) were to terminate in circumstances where a termination fee of $164 million or $212 million became payable to the company, the benefit to ALLETE shareholder funds would be around $2.82 to $3.65 per share.

Updated and New Rate of Return Projections

Rate of return through the date of close of the proposed transaction will be driven by share price at date of purchase, subsequent dividend receipts, the dollar uplift between share purchase price and the $67 share sale price, and the duration of the investment between purchase date and the date of cash receipt following the close date. The longer the duration of the investment, the greater the dilution of rate of return due to uplift of the share price.

Updated Rate of Return Projections

At the time of my previous article, the expected rate of return of ~11% assumed a share purchase price of $65.62 and a transaction close by June 30, 2025. Given the advice from the company the MPUC hearing is not expected until later this fall, I am now assuming transaction close will not occur ahead of say October 23, 2025, 30 days after commencement of this fall. This is interesting, as in the normal course of events, an increased quarterly dividend would be declared around October 25, 2025. I have extended the Table 1 spreadsheet calculations from my previous article out to 31 December 2025. If the $67 cash price was settled on October 23, ahead of the October 25 dividend declaration, a rate of return of 6.67% would be achieved from a purchase of ALLETE stock at market price of $65.62 on Feb. 7, 2025. Settlement a month later on November 25, when the stock had gone Ex-Div would increase the rate of return to 7.21%. Settlement on 31 December 2025 would result in a lower rate of return of 6.50% due to the longer duration of the investment.

New Rate of Return Projections - Current Share Purchase

From my previous article -

"Don't expect the share price to exactly track Table 1 daily balances - but that presents opportunities"

The indicated return of ~11% per previous article will not be achieved because the company has subsequently advised the anticipated mid-year close will not be met. However, the company is indicating close of the transaction is still expected in 2025. The market has obviously reacted to the deferral of the anticipated close date, with a fall in the share price to the current $63.53 at close on 20 June 2025. This provides an opportunity for a rate of return of 14.18% from a current purchase of ALLETE shares based on settlement of the proposed $67 per share occurring on 31 December 2025 (see Table 1 below). Of course, the risk of transaction close being further deferred beyond the end of 2025 remains. As discussed above, earlier settlement of the $67 per share would result in a higher rate of return than 14.18% due to the shorter duration of investment.

Table 1

Table 1 above shows how a purchase of ALLETE stock at the current price of $63.53 per share could be expected to provide ~14.18% return assuming the proposed $67 per share is received on 31 December 2025.

Think of Table 1 as a bank statement, with an interest rate of 14.18% per year compounding on a daily basis. A deposit of $63.53 today, with two withdrawals of $0.73 each as indicated, and the daily balances and ending balance of $67.00 as shown in Table 1 will be the result (note that interest calculations in Table 1 take account of dividend receipt occurring later than ex-div date).

Don't expect the share price to exactly track Table 1 daily balances - but that presents opportunities

Table 1 above shows the projected ALLETE share price assuming uniform growth at the 14.18% rate. But that is not how markets behave. If on any day in the future the share price falls below the price for that day indicated in Table 1, that could be an opportunity to buy for a better return than buying at the current price, provided of course, the expected settlement date and other assumptions do not change.

ALLETE: Summary and Conclusions

The share price at close on June 20, 2025, was $63.53, and if the sale proceeds as currently indicated, a payment of $67 per share will be made to shareholders by the end of 2025. The company has indicated dividends will continue to be paid in the interim, so dividends totaling at least $1.46 will also be received (there could be an increase in the quarterly dividend rate based on past actions). A shareholder could sell now for $63.53 and put the proceeds into a bank interest-bearing cash deposit. Alternatively, a shareholder could hold to receive two dividends of at least $0.73 each per share and wait for the payment of $67 per share, by 31 December 2025. As per Table 1 above, the equivalent daily compounding interest rate required from a bank cash deposit would need to be ~14.18% per year to match the potential return from the shareholder's ALLETE shares. That appears to be a fairly good return for what is likely a fairly low-risk option. A similar return could be earned by anyone buying ALLETE shares on the market at $63.53. Accordingly, I maintain a Hold recommendation for existing shareholders, and ALLETE would be a Strong Buy for potential new investors and existing investors looking for a fairly assured ~14.18% per year daily compounding return, for an investment maturing in ~6 months time. In the highly unlikely event the transaction does not close, stockholders would be left with shares in a solid utility company, with a dividend yield of 4.56%, and a forward P/E ratio of 17.00 (Sector median per SA Premium 17.59). Table 1 also provides prices payable on future dates which would achieve a similar ~14.18% return, although for a shorter duration of investment. Purchase of ALLETE stock at actual share prices below the share price for any particular day shown in Table 1 would result in returns greater than 14.18%, and vice versa. There is the risk the transaction might not close, either due to failure to achieve all regulatory approvals, or failure of the buyers to complete, but there appears to be no indication at present that either of these eventualities might occur. The greater risk could be a further delay in close beyond the end of 2025, and any increase in duration of investment would result in a reduction of the potential rate of return, as has already occurred since the date of the previous article linked above.
